title: Managing an older laying flock
subtitle: When to cull, when to keep, and how to maintain production as hens age.
excerpt: A hybrid hen peaks in her first year and declines steadily after that. By year three, most hybrids are laying at half their first-year rate. Managing an older flock means making regular decisions about which hens earn their keep, how to integrate new birds, and when the flock as a whole needs replacing.
sourceNotes: Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, flock management chapters. DEFRA/APHA poultry welfare codes. Cornell Small Farms laying-hen management guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A commercial cross-bred hen such as the ISA Brown or Bovans Brown, bred specifically for high first-year egg production.
A commercial term for a laying hen past peak production; in a smallholder context, a hen no longer laying enough to justify her feed.

content.text: produces roughly 300 eggs in her first year, around 200 in her second, and under 150 in her third. She also moulted each autumn, and each moult costs weeks of production. The practical question for a smallholder isn't whether to cull, it's when, and how to balance the age structure of the flock so you're never without eggs.

In a flock of ten or more hens, individual production is hard to track without individual identification. There are indirect signs: a hen with a pale, shrunken comb compared to her neighbours is likely low-producing or not laying at all. The vent check is more reliable: a laying hen has a moist, wide, oval vent; a non-laying hen has a dry, round, narrow vent. This is not definitive, a hen in moult has neither, but it's a quick guide.
Second-year hens are still worth keeping if feed costs are low, if the flock provides pest control or compost inputs beyond eggs, or if you value them as pets. Third-year hybrids are

by commercial standards but still lay periodically. Some smallholders keep them until they show signs of age-related illness; others cull at three years and replace with pullets. The choice depends on your priorities.

The simplest way to maintain year-round production is to run hens of at least two ages simultaneously. Replace half the flock with new pullets each year. That way, when the older birds go into moult in autumn, the pullets (who don't moult in their first winter) carry production through. If the whole flock is the same age, production gaps are inevitable.
Introducing new birds to an older flock

New pullets added to an established older flock are at the bottom of the pecking order and will be bullied, expected and temporary, but worth monitoring. A flock that is overcrowded makes integration harder. Give new birds a visual barrier, a screen or divider they can retreat behind, for the first week, then remove it once the hierarchy has settled.

Older hens carry higher loads of Mycoplasma, coccidial oocysts, and Marek's virus. Introducing unvaccinated young birds into a mixed-age flock exposes them to all of these. Vaccinate pullets for Marek's disease at day old (usually done by the hatchery) and quarantine new birds for four weeks before mixing.
